summary: Gruber juxtaposes Google VP Vic Gundotra's lofty rhetoric about Android being an open, choice-driven platform against Skyhook Wireless's lawsuit allegations that Google exercises tight, arbitrary control over Android device compliance and forces OEMs to use Google's location services. By bookending the piece with Gundotra's idealistic quotes and sandwiching damning legal complaint excerpts in between, Gruber lets the contradiction speak for itself. The post argues that Google's 'openness' narrative is undercut by its own behavior as a platform gatekeeper. The Skyhook filing suggests Google uses subjective compliance reviews to maintain control over the Android ecosystem in ways that mirror the very 'draconian' control Google accused Apple of wielding.
key_points: Google publicly positioned Android as the antidote to a 'draconian' future of one man, one phone, one carrier controlling mobile
Skyhook's lawsuit alleges Google uses subjective compliance reviews to arbitrarily block software and features from Android devices
Google allegedly requires OEMs to use Google Location Service, effectively creating a closed system despite open-source claims
The Skyhook filing directly echoes Google's own rhetoric back at them, noting Google ensures 'no industry player can restrict or control the innovations of any other, unless it is Google'
Gruber structures the piece as pure juxtaposition, letting the hypocrisy reveal itself without explicit commentary
money_quotes: If Google didn't act, it faced a draconian future where one man, one phone, one carrier were our choice. That's a future we don't want.

This entirely subjective review, conducted solely by Google employees with ultimate authority to interpret the scope and meaning of the CDD as they see fit, effectively gives Google the ability to arbitrarily deem any software, feature or function 'non-compatible' with the CDD.

Though Google claims the Android OS is open source, by requiring OEMs to use Google Location Service, an application that is inextricably bundled with the OS level framework, Google is effectively creating a closed system with respect to location positioning.
Google's manipulation suggests that the true purpose of Android is, or has become, to ensure that 'no industry player can restrict or control the innovations of any other', unless it is Google.
If you believe in openness, if you believe in choice, if you believe in innovation from everyone, then welcome to Android.
key_insight: Google's rhetoric about Android's openness is contradicted by its own gatekeeping practices, which mirror the very control it accused Apple of exercising.
